title: Study Finds Two Existing Drugs May Reverse Fatty Liver Disease
summary: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD), commonly known as fatty liver disease, affects about one in three adults globally, leading to liver damage and increased cardiovascular risk. A study from the University of Barcelona suggests that repurposing two existing drugs, pemafibrate and telmisartan, could offer a breakthrough. Researchers tested these drugs in animal models, including rats and zebrafish, and found that they significantly reduced liver fat buildup caused by a high-fat, high-fructose diet. Using the drugs together in lower doses was as effective as higher doses alone, potentially reducing toxicity and improving safety. The drugs, already approved for cardiovascular conditions, may also lower blood pressure and cholesterol, addressing related health risks. This approach aims to prevent disease progression in early stages, leveraging the safety of established medications to provide a faster and more cost-effective treatment option.

background_read: Fatty liver disease, now called MASLD, is a widespread condition where fat accumulates in the liver, often due to poor diet and lifestyle. It can progress to serious liver damage and increase the risk of heart disease. Current treatments are limited, and many experimental drugs fail in trials due to safety issues. Drug repurposing involves using approved medications for new purposes, which can speed up development and reduce risks. This study focuses on early-stage MASLD, aiming to prevent advancement to severe stages by testing safe, existing drugs in animal models to assess their effectiveness in reducing liver fat and cardiovascular risks.
Article_Structure: Main Points: The study shows that pemafibrate and telmisartan reduce liver fat in animal models of MASLD, with combination therapy offering synergistic effects and lower toxicity. Purpose: To explore drug repurposing for early-stage MASLD, providing a safer and faster treatment option. Evidence Evaluation: Based on animal experiments with rats and zebrafish, using controlled diets to induce disease, but human trials are needed for confirmation. Author Credibility: Led by Marta Alegret, a professor at the University of Barcelona, with collaborations from reputable institutions like CIBEROBN and Uppsala University. Methodology: Researchers tested drugs in animal models fed high-fat, high-fructose diets, measuring liver fat reduction and assessing cardiovascular markers.

perspectives.description: The research highlights drug repurposing as a promising approach to address MASLD with existing safe medications.
